questions.promptTex: \text{A triangle has area } A = 24 \text{ and semiperimeter } s = 8. \\ \text{Find its inradius } r.
hints: The single most important formula about the incircle: $r = A / s$.
Just plug in the numbers.

steps.text: **Apply the inradius formula** — area divided by semiperimeter:

questions.prompt: The incenter of a triangle is the point of concurrency of which three lines?
choices: The three medians
The three altitudes
The three angle bisectors
The three perpendicular bisectors of the sides
hints: What property must the center of the inscribed circle have? It must be equidistant from all three sides.
Which line has the property of being equidistant from two sides of an angle?
questions.explanation: Each angle bisector is the set of points equidistant from the two sides forming that angle. The three bisectors meet at one point equidistant from **all three** sides — that's the incenter. (For reference: medians → centroid, altitudes → orthocenter, perpendicular bisectors → circumcenter.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of an equilateral triangle with side length } 6. \\ \text{Give a decimal rounded to 2 places.}
hints: For an equilateral triangle with side $a$: $r = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{6}$.
Or compute $A$ and $s$ separately: $A = (\sqrt{3}/4)a^2$, $s = 3a/2$, then $r = A/s$.
questions.explanation: $r = 6\sqrt{3}/6 = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$.

steps.text: **Compute area** of an equilateral triangle with side $a = 6$:

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of the 6-8-10 right triangle.}
hints: 10 is the hypotenuse (longest side, opposite the right angle).
Right-triangle shortcut: $r = (a + b - c)/2$, where $c$ is the hypotenuse.

steps.text: **Spot the right angle.** Sides 6, 8, 10 satisfy $6^2 + 8^2 = 100 = 10^2$, so this is a right triangle with legs 6 and 8 and hypotenuse 10.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,160 200,160 40,40' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='150' width='10' height='10' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='120' r='40' fill='rgba(34,197,94,0.18)' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='120' r='3' fill='#15803d'/><text x='86' y='118' font-size='11' fill='#15803d' font-weight='700'>I</text><text x='120' y='178' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>8</text><text x='22' y='102' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>6</text><text x='130' y='90' font-size='13' fill='#1e3a8a' font-weight='700' transform='rotate(-37 130 90)'>10</text></svg>

steps.text: **Use the right-triangle shortcut.** For legs $a$, $b$ and hypotenuse $c$:
**Sanity check with the general formula.** Area $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 8 = 24$. Semiperimeter $= (6+8+10)/2 = 12$. So $r = 24/12 = 2$. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of the 5-12-13 right triangle.}
hints: Recognize the 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple — it's a right triangle.
Use the shortcut $r = (a + b - c)/2$ with $c = 13$.

steps.text: **5-12-13 is a Pythagorean triple.** $5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169 = 13^2$, so it's a right triangle with legs $5, 12$ and hypotenuse $13$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of the isosceles triangle with sides } 13, 13, 10. \\ \text{Give an exact decimal (rounded to 2 places).}
hints: Drop an altitude from the apex (between the two equal sides) to the base of length 10. It bisects the base.
The altitude has length $\sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = 12$ (a 5-12-13 right triangle pops out!). Then area = (1/2)(10)(12) = 60.

steps.text: **Find the altitude.** Drop a perpendicular from the apex to the base of length 10. It bisects the base into two segments of length 5 each. This creates a right triangle with hypotenuse $13$ and one leg $5$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of an equilateral triangle with side length } 12. \\ \text{Give a decimal rounded to 2 places.}
hints: Equilateral inradius formula: $r = a\sqrt{3}/6$.
$12\sqrt{3}/6 = 2\sqrt{3}$. Approximate $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle has vertices } A(0,0),\; B(8,0),\; C(0,6). \\ \text{Find the coordinates of the incenter.}
hints: Compute side lengths first. $a = |BC|$ (opposite $A$), $b = |AC|$ (opposite $B$), $c = |AB|$ (opposite $C$).
Then use the weighted-average formula $I = (a\,A + b\,B + c\,C) / (a+b+c)$.

steps.text: **Find the three side lengths.** Use the distance formula.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='30' y1='30' x2='30' y2='180' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='220' y2='180' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><polygon points='30,180 190,180 30,60' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='30' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='28' y='195' font-size='11' text-anchor='end'>A(0,0)</text><circle cx='190' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='195' y='184' font-size='11'>B(8,0)</text><circle cx='30' cy='60' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='28' y='56' font-size='11' text-anchor='end'>C(0,6)</text><circle cx='70' cy='140' r='14' fill='rgba(34,197,94,0.18)' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='70' cy='140' r='3' fill='#15803d'/><text x='76' y='138' font-size='11' fill='#15803d' font-weight='700'>I</text></svg>

steps.text: **Apply the incenter coordinate formula** (weighted by opposite side lengths):
**Sanity check.** This is a 6-8-10 right triangle, so $r = (6+8-10)/2 = 2$. The incenter at $(2,2)$ is at perpendicular distance $2$ from both the x-axis and y-axis — consistent with $r = 2$. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the inradius of the triangle with sides } 7, 8, 9. \\ \text{Give a decimal rounded to 2 places.}
hints: Not a right triangle, so use Heron's formula for the area.
$s = 12$, area $= \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)} = \sqrt{12 \cdot 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3}$.
questions.explanation: $s = 12$. Area $= \sqrt{12 \cdot 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3} = \sqrt{720} = 12\sqrt{5}$. $r = 12\sqrt{5}/12 = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$.

questions.promptTex: \text{The incircle of } \triangle ABC \text{ touches side } BC \text{ at point } X. \\ \text{Given } BX = 4,\; XC = 5,\; \text{ and } AB = 7,\; \text{ find } AC.
hints: Tangent segments from a single external point to a circle are equal. So $BX = BZ$ (where $Z$ is the tangent point on $AB$), and similarly $CX = CY$ on $AC$, and $AY = AZ$ on the two sides through $A$.
From $AB = 7$ and $BZ = 4$: $AZ = 3$. So $AY = 3$. Then $AC = AY + YC = 3 + 5$.
questions.explanation: Tangent lengths from each vertex are equal. From $B$: $BX = BZ = 4$. So $AZ = AB - BZ = 7 - 4 = 3$. From $A$: $AY = AZ = 3$. From $C$: $CY = CX = 5$. Therefore $AC = AY + YC = 3 + 5 = 8$.

steps.text: **Set up the tangent points.** Let the incircle touch $BC$ at $X$, $CA$ at $Y$, and $AB$ at $Z$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='50,170 250,170 70,30' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='110' cy='130' r='28' fill='rgba(34,197,94,0.18)' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='110' cy='130' r='3' fill='#15803d'/><text x='116' y='128' font-size='11' fill='#15803d' font-weight='700'>I</text><circle cx='130' cy='170' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='130' y='188' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>X</text><circle cx='86' cy='102' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='70' y='106' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>Z</text><circle cx='140' cy='92' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='148' y='90' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>Y</text><text x='44' y='180' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>B</text><text x='254' y='180' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>C</text><text x='62' y='28' font-size='13' font-weight='700'>A</text><text x='90' y='184' font-size='11'>4</text><text x='190' y='184' font-size='11'>5</text></svg>

steps.text: **Use the tangent-equality fact.** Two tangent segments from a single external point to a circle have equal length. So tangents from $B$ satisfy $BX = BZ$. From $C$: $CX = CY$. From $A$: $AY = AZ$.
**Find $AZ$ on side $AB$.** Side $AB$ is split into $AZ + ZB = AB$:

questions.promptTex: \text{A triangle has inradius } r = 3 \text{ and perimeter } 20. \\ \text{Find its area.}
hints: Rearrange $r = A/s$ for $A$.
Perimeter is $2s$, so $s = 10$.

steps.text: **The inradius formula** rearranges to $A = r \cdot s$ — area equals inradius times semiperimeter.

questions.promptTex: \text{An equilateral triangle has area } 9\sqrt{3}. \\ \text{Find the area of its inscribed circle. (Express as } k\pi \text{ and give } k.)
hints: First find the side length $a$ from the area formula $A = (\sqrt{3}/4)a^2$.
Then $r = a\sqrt{3}/6$, and the circle's area is $\pi r^2$.
questions.explanation: $a = 6$ (from $9\sqrt{3} = (\sqrt{3}/4)a^2$). $r = 6\sqrt{3}/6 = \sqrt{3}$. Incircle area $= \pi (\sqrt{3})^2 = 3\pi$. So $k = 3$.

steps.text: **Find the side length $a$.** The equilateral area formula:

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='30,170 210,170 120,15' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='117' r='35' fill='rgba(34,197,94,0.22)' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='120' cy='117' r='3' fill='#15803d'/><line x1='120' y1='117' x2='120' y2='170' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.8'/><text x='126' y='148' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>r</text><text x='120' y='192' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>side = 6</text></svg>

questions.prompt: Why do the three angle bisectors of any triangle CONCUR (meet at a single point)?
choices: Coincidence — only works for special triangles

Each angle bisector is the LOCUS of points equidistant from the two sides forming that angle; intersect two of them and the resulting point is equidistant from all three sides — so it lies on the third bisector too
Because the angles of a triangle sum to 180°
It's only true for acute triangles
hints: Think about what the bisector of ∠A IS, geometrically — what is the defining property of its points?
Distance from a point to a line is well-defined. Equidistance from two lines = on the angle bisector between them.
questions.explanation: The bisector of $\angle A$ is the locus of points P with $\text{dist}(P, AB) = \text{dist}(P, AC)$. Intersect the bisector of $\angle A$ with the bisector of $\angle B$: the meeting point has dist from $AB$ = dist from $AC$ AND dist from $AB$ = dist from $BC$. So all three distances are equal — that point lies on the bisector of $\angle C$ too.
steps: **Angle bisector's defining property.** $P$ lies on the bisector of $\angle A$ ⇔ $P$ is equidistant from the two sides $AB$ and $AC$.

**Intersect two bisectors.** Let $I$ = intersection of the bisectors of $\angle A$ and $\angle B$. Then $\text{dist}(I, AB) = \text{dist}(I, AC)$ (bisector of $A$) AND $\text{dist}(I, AB) = \text{dist}(I, BC)$ (bisector of $B$). Combining: $I$ is equidistant from all three sides.
**Third bisector forced.** Since $\text{dist}(I, AC) = \text{dist}(I, BC)$, $I$ also lies on the bisector of $\angle C$. Three bisectors concurrent ⇒ incenter exists and is unique.

**Pattern lesson.** Same reasoning shape as the circumcenter proof: each construction has a defining 'equidistance' property; intersecting two of them inherits the property, forcing the third. Geometry's concurrence theorems often reduce to this trick.

questions.prompt: For which triangle shapes does the INCENTER lie inside the triangle?
choices: Only acute triangles
Acute and right triangles only — not obtuse
ALL triangles — the incenter is always inside
Only equilateral triangles
hints: The incircle is inscribed inside the triangle, tangent to all three sides. Its center is at the center of the inscribed circle.
If the circle is inside, where must its center be?
questions.explanation: The incircle, by definition, is the circle INSIDE the triangle tangent to all three sides. Its center — the incenter — is therefore always inside the triangle. Unlike the circumcenter and orthocenter (which can escape outside for obtuse triangles), the incenter never leaves the interior.
steps: **Definition of the incircle.** The unique circle inside the triangle tangent to all three sides. Its center is the incenter.
**Inside-ness of the center.** A circle's center is always inside the circle, and the incircle is inside the triangle. By transitivity, the incenter is inside the triangle. No exceptions.

**Summary table of triangle centers.**

| Center | Always inside? |
|---|---|
| Centroid $G$ | ✓ always (average of vertices) |
| Incenter $I$ | ✓ always (center of inscribed circle) |
| Circumcenter $O$ | only for acute |
| Orthocenter $H$ | only for acute |

**Pattern lesson.** Centers built from internal constructions (averages, inscribed objects) stay inside. Centers built from perpendicular constructions (perpendicular bisectors, altitudes) can escape outside.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with semiperimeter } s = 15 \text{ and side } a = 8 \text{ opposite } \angle A = 60°. \\ \text{Find the inradius using the formula } r = (s - a) \tan(A/2). \\ \text{Round to 2 decimal places.}
questions.explanation: An alternative inradius formula: $r = (s - a) \tan(A/2)$. With $s = 15$, $a = 8$, $A = 60°$: $r = 7 \cdot \tan(30°) = 7/\sqrt{3} = 7\sqrt{3}/3 \approx 4.04$.
steps: **Recognize the formula.** $r = (s - a)\tan(A/2)$ relates the inradius to the side $a$, the semiperimeter $s$, and the half-angle at $A$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,30 40,170 240,170" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="140" cy="130" r="32" fill="rgba(34,197,94,0.18)" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="140" cy="130" r="3" fill="#15803d"/><line x1="140" y1="130" x2="140" y2="170" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/><rect x="134" y="160" width="6" height="6" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.1"/><path d="M 130 50 A 25 25 0 0 1 150 50" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.4"/><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="40" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="240" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="22" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="26" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="246" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="125" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-style="italic">A/2</text><text x="146" y="134" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#15803d">I</text><text x="148" y="152" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">r</text><text x="126" y="185" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">a (opposite A)</text></svg>

steps: **Plug in.** $r = (15 - 8) \cdot \tan(30°) = 7 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{7\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx 4.04$.

**Where this formula comes from.** Drop a perpendicular from $I$ to side $b$ (or $c$). It forms a right triangle with leg $r$ (the inradius), leg $s - a$ (a well-known 'tangent-length' identity), and angle $A/2$ at vertex $A$. So $\tan(A/2) = r / (s - a)$ — rearranging gives the formula. Pattern lesson: classical inradius formulas drop out of small right triangles formed by the incircle and a vertex angle bisector.

questions.promptTex: \text{In } \triangle ABC, \angle A = 50°. \\ \text{Find } \angle BIC \text{ (the angle at the incenter looking at side } BC).
hints: Classical formula: $\angle BIC = 90° + A/2$.
questions.explanation: A beautiful classical identity: $\angle BIC = 90° + \dfrac{A}{2}$. With $A = 50°$: $\angle BIC = 90° + 25° = 115°$. Always between $90°$ and $135°$ for non-degenerate triangles.
steps: **State the identity.** $\angle BIC = 90° + \dfrac{A}{2}$. The angle at the incenter opposite vertex $A$ is always obtuse.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,30 40,170 240,170" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="140" cy="130" r="32" fill="rgba(34,197,94,0.18)" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="140" y1="130" x2="40" y2="170" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.4"/><line x1="140" y1="130" x2="240" y2="170" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.4"/><circle cx="140" cy="130" r="4" fill="#15803d"/><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="40" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="240" cy="170" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="22" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="26" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="246" y="185" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="134" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#15803d">I</text><text x="108" y="152" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-style="italic">∠BIC</text></svg>

steps: **Why is it true?** In $\triangle BIC$, the angles are $\angle IBC = B/2$, $\angle ICB = C/2$, and $\angle BIC$. They sum to $180°$: $\angle BIC = 180° - B/2 - C/2 = 180° - (B+C)/2$. But $B + C = 180° - A$, so $\angle BIC = 180° - (180° - A)/2 = 90° + A/2$.
**Sanity check.** As $A \to 0°$: $\angle BIC \to 90°$ (right angle limit, degenerate triangle). As $A \to 180°$: $\angle BIC \to 180°$ (incenter slides to vertex $A$, degenerate).

questions.prompt: Euler's inequality: for any triangle, the inradius r and circumradius R satisfy:
choices: $r = R$ — they're always equal
$r \geq R$ — inradius is at least the circumradius
$r \leq R/2$, with equality only for equilateral triangles
There's no general relation
hints: An equilateral triangle has $r/R = 1/2$ exactly — that's the EXTREME case.
All other triangles have $r/R < 1/2$. Equivalently $2r \leq R$.
questions.explanation: $r \leq R/2$ for every triangle, with equality iff the triangle is equilateral (Euler, 1765). Often written as $R \geq 2r$. The further a triangle is from equilateral, the more $r$ shrinks compared to $R$.
steps: **Statement.** Euler's classical inequality: $r \leq \dfrac{R}{2}$.
**Equality case.** Holds with equality iff the triangle is equilateral. Any other triangle has a strictly smaller $r/R$ ratio.

**Beautiful proof sketch.** Use the identity $OI^2 = R^2 - 2Rr$ (Euler's formula, where $O$ is the circumcenter and $I$ is the incenter). Since $OI^2 \geq 0$, we get $R^2 \geq 2Rr$, i.e. $r \leq R/2$. Equality ⇔ $O = I$ ⇔ equilateral.

**Pattern lesson.** Many classical inequalities have one extremal triangle (often equilateral). Recognizing this lets you guess the equality case before proving it — a useful problem-solving heuristic.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with incenter } I, \text{ inradius } r = 3, \text{ and angle } \angle A = 60°. \\ \text{Find } |AI|.
hints: Drop a perpendicular from $I$ to side $AB$ (or $AC$); it has length $r$. This creates a right triangle with hypotenuse $AI$ and angle $A/2$ at $A$.
$\sin(A/2) = r / |AI|$, so $|AI| = r / \sin(A/2)$.
questions.explanation: The incenter lies on the bisector of $\angle A$, so the line $AI$ makes angle $A/2$ with each side at $A$. Dropping a perpendicular from $I$ to side $AB$ (length $r$) gives a right triangle with hypotenuse $|AI|$ and opposite leg $r$: $\sin(A/2) = r/|AI| \Rightarrow |AI| = r/\sin(A/2) = 3/\sin(30°) = 6$.
steps: **Set up the right triangle.** Drop a perpendicular from $I$ to side $AB$ (or $AC$). It has length $r$ (the inradius). $AI$ is the hypotenuse, and the angle at $A$ inside this little triangle is $A/2$ (because $AI$ bisects $\angle A$).

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="40,30 40,180 230,180" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="95" cy="148" r="32" fill="rgba(34,197,94,0.18)" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="95" y2="148" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.6"/><line x1="40" y1="148" x2="95" y2="148" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/><rect x="40" y="142" width="6" height="6" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.1"/><path d="M 40 60 A 30 30 0 0 1 56 79" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.3"/><circle cx="95" cy="148" r="4" fill="#15803d"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="22" y="30" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="102" y="152" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#15803d">I</text><text x="58" y="72" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">A/2</text><text x="58" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">AI</text><text x="56" y="140" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-style="italic">r</text></svg>

steps: **Apply $\sin$.** $\sin(A/2) = \dfrac{r}{|AI|} \;\Longrightarrow\; |AI| = \dfrac{r}{\sin(A/2)}$.
**Mirror identities at $B, C$.** $|BI| = r/\sin(B/2)$, $|CI| = r/\sin(C/2)$. Together with $|AI|$ these tie the incenter to each vertex via the half-angles.

questions.prompt: Is the incenter I generally on the Euler line of the triangle (the line through O, G, H)?
choices: Yes — I is always on the Euler line
No — in general, I lies OFF the Euler line. (For equilateral triangles all four centers coincide, but that's a degenerate special case where the Euler line is undefined.)
Yes, for right triangles
Yes, for acute triangles
hints: The Euler line connects O, G, H — but I is a different kind of center, built from angle bisectors, not perpendicular constructions.
Generically there are FOUR distinct classical centers; only three are collinear.
questions.explanation: For a typical (non-equilateral) triangle, the incenter lies OFF the Euler line. The Euler line is special to the trio $\{O, G, H\}$. The incenter $I$, built from angle bisectors, doesn't share their alignment. For an equilateral triangle, all four centers collapse to one point and the Euler line is undefined.
steps: **Why $O, G, H$ are collinear.** Direct vector identity: $\vec{OH} = 3 \vec{OG}$. This identity falls out of the centroid being the centroid of $\{A, B, C\}$ AND being the centroid of $\{A, B, C, H\}$ on the right scaling. It's specific to these three centers.

**Why $I$ is generally separate.** The incenter is the weighted average $I = (aA + bB + cC)/(a+b+c)$. The weights are side lengths, not equal weights. There's no general identity placing $I$ on the line through $O, G, H$.

**Pattern lesson.** Triangle geometry has many 'centers' (over 50,000 catalogued in the Encyclopedia of Triangle Centers). Most pairs of centers don't have a simple linear relationship. The Euler-line trio is exceptional.

questions.promptTex: \text{In an equilateral triangle, find the ratio } r / R \\ \text{(inradius to circumradius).}
hints: For an equilateral with side $a$: $r = a\sqrt{3}/6$ and $R = a\sqrt{3}/3$.
questions.explanation: For an equilateral triangle with side $a$, the inradius is $r = a\sqrt{3}/6$ and the circumradius is $R = a\sqrt{3}/3 = 2r$. So $r/R = 1/2$ exactly. This is the EQUALITY case of Euler's inequality $r \leq R/2$ — equilateral triangles achieve the bound.
steps: **Recall the equilateral formulas.** For side $a$: $r = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{6}$, $R = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{3}$.
**Take the ratio.** $\dfrac{r}{R} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}/6}{a\sqrt{3}/3} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

**Connection to Euler's inequality.** $r \leq R/2$ for every triangle, with equality ⇔ equilateral. The equilateral triangle is the *maximally efficient* shape — it gets the largest possible inscribed circle relative to its circumscribed circle.

**Pattern lesson.** Equilateral triangles are local extremals for many geometric quantities: $r/R$, area-to-perimeter² ratio, etc. When a question asks 'over all triangles, when is X extremal?', equilateral is the first guess.
